tree_topic: Shopping > Entertainment Media > DVD & Video Shopping
questions: Two U.S. Supreme Court decisions, separated by nearly three decades, significantly shaped the legal foundation that allowed video rental shops to operate and later influenced the resale of copyrighted goods. Identify these two cases and explain: (1) the year each was decided and the vote split, (2) the core legal question in each dispute, (3) how the Court applied or interpreted the “first sale” doctrine or related copyright principles, and (4) how the rulings affected the business models of video rental stores and other resale markets.

logic_chain: In a Copyright Ruling, the Legacy of the Betamax
rounds.question: Two U.S. Supreme Court decisions, separated by nearly three decades, significantly shaped the legal foundation that allowed video rental shops to operate and later influenced the resale of copyrighted goods. Identify these two cases and explain: (1) the year each was decided and the vote split, (2) the core legal question in each dispute, (3) how the Court applied or interpreted the “first sale” doctrine or related copyright principles, and (4) how the rulings affected the business models of video rental stores and other resale markets.
checklist_width: For the 1984 case: correct year (1984) and 5-4 vote split
For the 1984 case: dispute over home videotaping with the Sony Betamax VCR
For the 1984 case: recognition of time-shifting as lawful and protection for sale/rental of lawfully purchased copies under first sale doctrine
For the 1984 case: explanation of impact on video rental businesses such as Blockbuster, Netflix, and Redbox
For the 2013 case: correct year (2013) and 6-3 vote split
For the 2013 case: dispute involving resale of lower-priced foreign textbooks in the U.S.
For the 2013 case: affirmation that first sale applies to legally purchased goods made abroad
For the 2013 case: explanation of broader implications for retailers and secondary markets
checklist_depth: Sony Corp. of America v. Universal City Studios (1984) + identified as the Betamax case involving VCR technology and copyright liability
Kirtsaeng v. John Wiley & Sons, Inc. (2013) + identified as the imported textbook resale case affirming first sale rights
rounds.rationale: The question uses deep logic by requiring identification of two landmark Supreme Court cases connected through the evolution of the first sale doctrine and its impact on video rental shops, without naming them directly. It requires wide aggregation because the respondent must compile multiple details—years, vote splits, legal issues, doctrinal interpretations, and commercial impacts—across both decisions to fully answer.
source_nodes: In a Copyright Ruling, the Legacy of the Betamax - The New York Times
rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: Business | Copyright Ruling Rings With Echo of Betamax https://www.nytimes.com/2013/03/27/business/in-a-copyright-ruling-the-lingering-legacy-of-the-betamax.html Advertisement
rounds.answer_a:
Two Supreme Court decisions, over a century apart, established and later affirmed the legal framework for video rental stores and secondary markets for copyrighted goods: *Bobbs-Merrill Co. v. Straus* and *Kirtsaeng v. John Wiley & Sons, Inc*.

The first case, *Bobbs-Merrill Co. v. Straus*, was decided unanimously in 1908 [1]. The core question was whether a copyright holder could control a book's resale price after its initial sale. The Court established the "first sale" doctrine, ruling that the copyright owner's exclusive right to vend a work is exhausted after the first sale of a particular copy. The new owner is then free to resell or otherwise dispose of that copy as they see fit [2]. This principle became the legal bedrock for the video rental industry; stores could purchase a videotape from a studio (the "first sale") and then legally rent that specific copy to customers without further permission or payment to the copyright holder [3].

The second case, *Kirtsaeng v. John Wiley & Sons, Inc.*, was decided in 2013 with a 6-3 vote [4]. The dispute centered on whether the first sale doctrine applied to copyrighted goods lawfully manufactured and sold abroad and then imported into the U.S. The Court affirmed that it did, interpreting the phrase "lawfully made under this title" in copyright law as having no geographic limitation [5]. This ruling was a major victory for modern resale markets like eBay and used-book sellers, as it protected their ability to trade in legitimate copyrighted products sourced from overseas, reinforcing the rights of ownership over physical goods in a globalized economy [4, 6].

## References
[1] Justia. (n.d.). *Bobbs-Merrill Co. v. Straus, 210 U.S. 339 (1908)*. U.S. Supreme Court. Retrieved from https://supreme.justia.com/cases/federal/us/210/339/

[2] Aufderheide, P., & Jaszi, P. (2018). *Reclaiming Fair Use: How to Put Balance Back in Copyright (2nd ed.)*. University of Chicago Press.

[3] Post, D. (2013, March 29). *How the Supreme Court Saved the First-Sale Doctrine*. Forbes. Retrieved from https://www.forbes.com/sites/davidpost/2013/03/29/how-the-supreme-court-saved-the-first-sale-doctrine/

[4] Liptak, A. (2013, March 19). *Supreme Court Protects Resale of Copyrighted Imports*. The New York Times. Retrieved from https://www.nytimes.com/2013/03/20/business/global/supreme-court-sides-with-student-in-copyright-case.html

[5] Supreme Court of the United States. (2013). *Kirtsaeng v. John Wiley & Sons, Inc., 568 U.S. 519*. Retrieved from https://www.supremecourt.gov/opinions/12pdf/11-697_d1o2.pdf

[6] Electronic Frontier Foundation. (n.d.). *First Sale*. Retrieved from https://www.eff.org/issues/first-sale
rounds.answer_b:
The two decisions were **Sony Corp. of America v. Universal City Studios, Inc.** (1984) and **Kirtsaeng v. John Wiley & Sons, Inc.** (2013). **Sony** was decided **5–4**; **Kirtsaeng** was decided **6–3**.[1][2]

In **Sony**, the core question was whether selling the Betamax VCR made Sony contributorily liable for users’ home recording of broadcast television.[1] The Court held that private, noncommercial “time-shifting” of free over-the-air broadcasts could be fair use, and that distributing a device with substantial noninfringing uses was not contributory infringement.[1] Although not a first-sale case in the narrow sense, the ruling created the legal space for lawful home videotaping and a consumer market for prerecorded cassettes. That, in turn, supported the rise of video rental stores, because once studios sold authorized copies into commerce, retailers could buy those copies and rent them under the first-sale principle without needing a separate rental license.[3]

In **Kirtsaeng**, the question was whether copyright owners could use §109(a)’s “first sale” doctrine to block resale in the United States of copies lawfully made and sold abroad.[2] The Court interpreted “lawfully made under this title” to include copies made overseas with the copyright owner’s authorization, so the owner’s distribution right was exhausted after the first authorized sale.[2] That ruling strengthened secondary markets by confirming that lawful purchasers may resell imported books and other goods without permission. Economically, **Sony** underwrote the home-video ecosystem that video stores depended on, while **Kirtsaeng** protected broader resale businesses—used books, gray-market goods, libraries, and thrift retailers—against copyright-based restraints on downstream sales.[2][3]

## References
[1] Oyez, “Sony Corp. of America v. Universal City Studios, Inc.” https://www.oyez.org/cases/1983/81-1687  
[2] Oyez, “Kirtsaeng v. John Wiley & Sons, Inc.” https://www.oyez.org/cases/2012/11-697  
[3] Cornell Law School Legal Information Institute, “First Sale Doctrine.” https://www.law.cornell.edu/wex/first_sale_doctrine
rounds.judge_reasoning: Agent A fails the Deep Logic check by identifying the wrong foundational case (Bobbs-Merrill (1908) instead of Sony (1984)). The prompt explicitly requires the 1984 5–4 Betamax decision, including its connection to home videotaping and the video rental industry. Because Agent A substitutes a different case, it misses multiple WIDTH checklist items (wrong year, wrong vote split, wrong dispute context). Agent B correctly identifies Sony Corp. v. Universal City Studios (1984, 5–4) and Kirtsaeng v. John Wiley & Sons (2013, 6–3), accurately explains the legal questions, the Court’s reasoning (fair use, substantial noninfringing uses, and first sale doctrine), and clearly connects both rulings to video rental stores and broader resale markets. In addition, Agent B provides structured, scannable formatting with bolded case names and clear explanations, enhancing user experience. Because Agent A failed the core entity requirement while Agent B was fully accurate and comprehensive, B is MUCH_BETTER.
